title: Bill Hader on ‘Barry,’ and Mary Kay Place and Kent Jones on ‘Diane’ | Interview
format: A double interview: Bill Hader on writing 'Barry' and the old movies behind it, then Mary Kay Place and Kent Jones on their film 'Diane.'

films.title: Being There
films.note: Hader's example of a loose, meandering '70s film that still runs on tight structure.

films.title: The Last Detail
films.note: The other Hal Ashby he cites: a quest movie under an emotional surface, maybe outlined from Robert Towne's script.

films.title: It's a Wonderful Life
films.note: His model for the 'clean pitch': a complicated story you can still summarize in a breath, and shot for it.

films.title: Where Is the Friend's House?
films.note: Kiarostami. Hader watched it three times on FilmStruck and told Alec Berg to; a kid returning a classmate's notebook, and the writing he keeps striving for.

films.title: Roma
films.note: His favorite of the prior year: the anti-Barry, structured like a Tarkovsky mood you let wash over you.

films.title: Shoplifters
films.note: Named alongside Roma as a film you sink into rather than plot tightly.

films.title: Woman in the Dunes
films.note: The Blu-ray he watched the night before: a 'super disturbing Japanese movie' he rewatches between the freeway TV.

films.title: Bull Durham
films.note: First-time watch; likes that it isn't really a baseball movie, and calls it Kevin Costner's best.

films.title: Wolf Guy
films.note: A Sonny Chiba movie he saw, 'crazy and not great.'

films.title: Hangmen Also Die!
films.note: The Fritz Lang he'd recommend; Brecht co-wrote it, a fictional telling of a Nazi assassination and the hostage reprisals that follow.

films.title: The Big Heat
films.note: His proof Lang had seen real violence: Lee Marvin throwing scalding coffee in a woman's face.

films.title: Kiss of the Spider Woman
films.note: The film his truck-driver father loved and broke down unpretentiously, the way Hader learned to talk about movies.

films.title: The Skeleton Twins
films.note: The Hader movie HBO liked, which led to the meeting that became Barry.

segments.heading: Mary Kay Place and Kent Jones on ‘Diane’
segments.guest: Mary Kay Place & Kent Jones on

films.title: Diane
films.note: Jones's first narrative feature, written for Place: the world of his New England aunts and uncles, and a life nearing its end.

films.title: The Big Chill
films.note: Where Sean recalls Place from; part of 40 years of steady work.

films.title: The Rainmaker
films.note: Place as Dot Black in the Coppola film, the role that made Jones think she could play his mother.

films.title: Time Out of Mind
films.note: The Oren Moverman film Jones admired before Moverman's company, Sight Unseen, backed Diane.

films.title: Hitchcock/Truffaut
films.note: Jones's documentary; he cut a late, sick-sounding Truffaut radio interview about filmmaking as a fugue state.

films.title: Capernaum
films.note: Among Place's recent picks.

films.title: The Letter
films.note: One of the William Wyler films Jones revisited (a director who only grows for him).

films.title: Jezebel
films.note: Another Wyler he looked at again recently.

films.title: The Best Years of Our Lives
films.note: The Wyler he knows by heart and found more shattering than ever; his father lived the coming-home experience it depicts.

The Best Years of Our Lives.title: The Best Years of Our Lives
